notes.anchor: on the sixteenth before the Kalends of June
notes.note: A Roman date reckoned by inclusive backward counting from the Kalends (the 1st) of June, i.e. 17 May 44 BC. Cicero announces his departure for his ancestral home at Arpinum.

notes.anchor: my own pupil --- who is dining with me today
notes.note: Dolabella, Cicero's former son-in-law and rhetorical protege, whom Cicero calls his "pupil"; the bitter joke is that this pupil is now infatuated with Caesar's memory ("the man whom our Brutus wounded").

notes.anchor: the man whom our Brutus wounded
notes.note: A pointed periphrasis for the dead dictator Julius Caesar, struck down at the Ides; "our Brutus" is Marcus Brutus the Liberator.

notes.anchor: if Pompeius arrives with a strong army
notes.note: Sextus Pompeius, younger son of Pompey the Great, was at large in Spain and the western seas in 44 BC; Cicero fears that his arrival with a Republican army would reignite civil war.

notes.anchor: the Ides of March do not console me as much as they did before. For they contain a great flaw
notes.note: The assassination of Caesar on 15 March 44 BC; Cicero's recurring lament is that the deed killed the tyrant but spared the tyranny (the "great flaw"), leaving the Liberators exposed.

A legatio votiva ("free legation for the discharge of a vow") was a quasi-official commission letting a senator travel abroad on state expense under the pretext of fulfilling a religious vow; Cicero wanted one as a respectable cover for leaving an increasingly dangerous Rome.

notes.anchor: warned by many not to be in the Senate on the Kalends
notes.note: Rumours warned Cicero that armed men were being mustered for the Senate meeting of 1 June 44 BC, aimed at the Liberators and their sympathizers, making attendance unsafe.
